_comment: Original short medieval-parable placeholders, one per virtue pairing (28 total). Each is hand-authored as scaffolding for the prototype; production will replace these with live LLM-generated parables seeded by this same structure. The pairing key is alphabetical: 'compassion-honesty' not 'honesty-compassion'.
_schema: key: '<virtueA>-<virtueB>' (alphabetical). scene: shared setup. options[virtueId]: the answer that embodies that virtue.

compassion-honesty.scene: Thou art a healer. The dying man clutches thy sleeve and asks if he shall see the morning. Thou knowest he will not.

options.honesty: Tell him plainly, with thy hand upon his.
options.compassion: Promise him morning, that he may sleep without fear.

honesty-valor.scene: A captain of the guard hath fled an outnumbered fight, leaving his men. He asks thee in private if his honor stands.

options.honesty: Say what is true: thou didst run.
options.valor: Say nothing. Take up his sword thyself, that the next fight be answered.

honesty-justice.scene: Thou art a witness at trial. The accused is plainly guilty, but only thou knowest a fact that would acquit him on a technicality.

options.honesty: Tell every fact in thy keeping, and let the court decide.
options.justice: Tell only what serves the just outcome thou knowest is right.

honesty-sacrifice.scene: Soldiers come asking if thou hast seen the boy they hunt. He sleeps in thy cellar. He is innocent.

options.honesty: Tell them what thou knowest. The truth is not thine to bend.
options.sacrifice: Lie, and take whatever punishment falls when the lie is found.

honesty-honor.scene: Thou hast given thy word to keep a friend's secret. Now a stranger asks the very question that secret would answer.

options.honesty: Speak the secret, for thy word to truth is older than thy word to him.
options.honor: Refuse to answer. A vow is a vow.

honesty-spirituality.scene: Thou hast seen a vision at the shrine that thy mind cannot explain. The brothers ask: what didst thou see?

options.honesty: Describe it as it came, however strange, and trust them to weigh it.
options.spirituality: Hold the vision close. Some things are not meant for the mouths of others.

honesty-humility.scene: A traveler praises thee for a deed thou didst perform. He believes thee a great hero.

options.honesty: Tell him thy part, no more and no less. The deed was thine.
options.humility: Say only that any would have done the same, and let his eyes turn elsewhere.

compassion-valor.scene: A young soldier asks to ride with thee against a foe far beyond his strength. He will die if he comes.

options.compassion: Send him home to his mother. Bear the shame of his disappointment thyself.
options.valor: Take him at thy side. He came to be a soldier, not a son.

compassion-justice.scene: A starving thief is brought before thee. The law is clear: a hand for a loaf. Thou art the magistrate.

options.compassion: Pay for the loaf from thy own purse and send him home fed.
options.justice: Pronounce the sentence the law demands.

compassion-sacrifice.scene: A wounded stranger asks to travel with thee. Thy road is hard and thy provisions thin.

options.compassion: Walk with him. Share thy bread.
options.sacrifice: Give him thy bread entire, and walk apart at thine own hunger.

compassion-honor.scene: The man who killed thy brother lies at thy mercy, bleeding in the road. He begs for water.

options.compassion: Give him water. He is dying. That is enough.
options.honor: Bind his wound and bear him to a physician, that he may stand for thy brother's judgment.

compassion-spirituality.scene: A widow weeps at her husband's grave and asks thee whether the soul lives on. Thou knowest only what thou hast been taught.

options.compassion: Tell her that he watches her. Let her sleep tonight.
options.spirituality: Tell her thou dost not know — and sit with her while she weeps.

compassion-humility.scene: A poor woman has nothing to give but her last loaf. She presses it upon thee in thanks for what thou hast done.

options.compassion: Take the loaf and eat it before her, that she may know her gift is whole.
options.humility: Refuse softly, and say her gift is too great for so small a deed.

justice-valor.scene: A bandit who has terrorized the road begs for trial in the city, not the noose at the wayside.

options.valor: End it here. He is judged by his deeds.
options.justice: Bind him and bring him to the city. Every man stands before a judge.

sacrifice-valor.scene: Thy company flees a battle lost. Someone must hold the bridge while the rest cross. Thy captain looks at thee.

options.valor: Stand on the bridge with thy sword. Many shall remember thy stand.
options.sacrifice: Volunteer before he asks. Let none know thy name when the song is sung.

honor-valor.scene: A drunken oaf at the tavern slurs thy lady's name. Thy hand is on thy sword. The room watches.

options.valor: Draw. Make him answer for what he hath said.
options.honor: Take his measure. A drunkard's tongue is no fit blade for thine. Walk past.

spirituality-valor.scene: Thou hast vowed to walk the pilgrim's road unarmed. On the road, a child is set upon by a wolf — and a passing hunter is already drawing his bow.

options.valor: Break the vow. Take up a fallen branch and go to the child.
options.spirituality: Trust the hunter to his shot. Kneel where thou art, and pray for the child.

humility-valor.scene: At the tournament thou hast bested every comer save one. He is a friend, and the crowd waits.

options.valor: Strike thy best blow. He came to be tested, as didst thou.
options.humility: Yield the field to him. Let the crown sit lighter than the friendship.

justice-sacrifice.scene: A merchant owes a great debt to a man who cheated him long ago. The court will collect it if thou dost speak.

options.justice: Speak. The debt is owed. The law does not weigh old wrongs.
options.sacrifice: Pay the debt thyself from thy own purse. Let neither man be wounded twice.

honor-justice.scene: Thy lord hath ordered an unjust eviction. The peasants beg thee, his bailiff, to refuse.

options.justice: Refuse. The law is older than thy lord.
options.honor: Carry out the order. Thy oath was sworn before any peasant knocked.

justice-spirituality.scene: A wandering monk preaches a doctrine the council has named heresy. He is gentle, and clearly devout.

options.justice: Bring him before the council. The law has been spoken.
options.spirituality: Walk with him a while. Listen. Then decide what is to be done.

humility-justice.scene: A child has stolen an apple from thy cart. He cries before thee, ashamed.

options.justice: Demand the price of the apple in labor. The rule is the rule.
options.humility: Give him the apple, and another for his sister. Pretend thou didst not see.

honor-sacrifice.scene: Thy friend is held by a cruel lord. The price of his release is thy public confession to a crime thou didst not commit.

options.honor: Refuse. Thy name is not thine to spend, even for him.

sacrifice-spirituality.scene: Thou art kept all night at a shrine in vigil. Outside, a beggar cries in the cold, and no other will rise.

options.sacrifice: Break the vigil and bring him in. Bear the priest's anger when dawn comes.
options.spirituality: Hold the vigil. Pray for him by name through the long night.

humility-sacrifice.scene: Thou hast given the last of thy harvest to feed thy village. The village wishes to crown thee its mayor.

options.sacrifice: Accept the burden. They will lean on thee in winter.
options.humility: Refuse the crown. The grain was theirs first; thou didst only carry it.

honor-spirituality.scene: Thou hast broken a vow. To confess is to lose thy knighthood; to seek another path is to keep thy peace privately.

options.honor: Confess publicly. The vow was made before others; so must its breaking be.
options.spirituality: Go to the shrine alone. Make thy peace with the one who heard the vow first.

honor-humility.scene: A young king hath wronged thee gravely and now stands before thee with bowed head, asking pardon in his court.

options.honor: Accept his apology formally, as becomes the gravity of his offense.
options.humility: Kneel beside him. He is the wounded one now.

humility-spirituality.scene: Thou hast received a vision the brothers would call a sign of high favor. To speak of it is to be made great among them.

options.spirituality: Tell them. The vision was given, not earned — but it was given to thee.
options.humility: Tell no one. The vision was given that thou shouldst be quieter, not louder.
